Canada.fact: In 2024, the mining industry directly contributed CAD $111 billion (approx. USD $81.5 billion) to Canada's Gross Domestic Product (GDP), representing 3.6% of the national economy.
The mining and mineral processing sector directly employs 438,000 workers and supports an additional 272,000 indirect jobs.
The mining industry is one of the largest private-sector employers of Indigenous Peoples in Canada, employing 22,600 Indigenous workers (7.5% of total workforce).
Mining exports reached a record CAD $152 billion (approx. USD $111.6 billion) in 2024, representing 21% of all Canadian merchandise exports (51% to US).
Canadian production of transition minerals has declined since 2015: copper down 23%, nickel down 44%, and iron ore down 6%.
The inflation-adjusted GDP of the Canadian mining sector in 2024 remained at levels virtually identical to those of 2007, showing stagnant growth.

Ontario remains Canada's largest producer with a total mineral production value of CAD $13.5 billion (approx. USD $9.9 billion), followed by Quebec at CAD $12.8 billion (approx. USD $9.4 billion) and British Columbia at CAD $11.2 billion (approx. USD $8.2 billion).
Regulatory delays under the federal Impact Assessment Act (IAA) mean it takes an average of 12 to 15 years to bring a new critical mineral mine from discovery to production in Canada.
Ontario's Ring of Fire region suffers from a severe grid and all-weather road infrastructure deficit, blocking access to multi-billion dollar nickel, copper, and chromite deposits.
Remote off-grid mines face an average carbon tax liability of CAD $4.2 million (approx. USD $3.15 million) annually if they cannot transition from diesel generation to renewable microgrids.

Australia.fact: In the 2023-24 financial year, the minerals sector contributed a record AUD $59.4 billion (approx. USD $39.2 billion) in company taxes and royalties to Australian governments.
Over the past decade, the Australian minerals sector has contributed a cumulative total of AUD $394.6 billion (approx. USD $260.4 billion) in company taxes and royalties.
Over the last decade, Australia's resources sector generated a total of AUD $2.9 trillion (approx. USD $1.91 trillion) in export revenues.
The industry directly employed 303,300 workers in 2023-24 (87% permanent full-time), while the broader METS sector supports an additional 1.1 million jobs.
The minerals sector contributed AUD $569.1 million (approx. USD $375.6 million) in direct spending in Victoria and supported a growing mineral resources boom in the Northern Territory.
The national rollout of the Towards Sustainable Mining (TSM) Australia framework provides site-level metrics on safety, tailings, and water stewardship.
Proposed multi-employer bargaining and labor hire rule changes could cost the Australian mining sector AUD $1.2 billion (approx. USD $792 million) annually in lost productivity.
The Australian Government allocated AUD $105.9 million (approx. USD $69.9 million) to expedite environmental assessments under the EPBC Act using AI technologies.
The Critical Minerals Facility was expanded to AUD $4 billion (approx. USD $2.64 billion) to support downstream processing plants like nickel sulphate and lithium hydroxide refineries.

China.fact: In 2023, China's geological exploration investment reached RMB 115 billion (approx. USD $16.4 billion), resulting in 124 new deposits.
China's domestic reserves of transition minerals increased in 2023: lithium reserves grew by 24%, cobalt reserves increased by 11%, and nickel reserves increased by 8%.
China accounted for over 54% of global refined steel output in 2023, maintaining dominance in refining copper and aluminum.
Over 1,000 mines nationwide have been officially certified under the National Green Mine Registry established by the China Mining Association (CMA).
Chinese mining firms invested USD $8.2 billion in overseas mining projects (Southeast Asia, Africa, South America) under the Belt and Road Initiative in 2023.
China's import dependency remains high: 75% for iron ore, and 78% for copper concentrate, driving deep-earth exploration (below 1,000m) and 5G automated mine designs.
The Chinese government allocated RMB 4.5 billion (approx. USD $640 million) in 2023 for the ecological restoration of abandoned mines, reclaiming 32,000 hectares.

Switzerland.fact: Switzerland acts as a major global commodities trading hub, hosting over 500 trading firms that manage significant global metal and mineral transactions.
Swiss legislation mandates that commodity firms disclose all payments to governments exceeding CHF 100,000 (approx. USD $110,000) annually to enhance transparency.
Swiss commodity firms must comply with new supply chain due diligence regulations covering child labor, conflict minerals, and ESG risks across their supply networks.

Global.fact: ICMM's 25 member companies represent one-third of the global mining industry and are committed to the Nature Position Statement and its 5-Point Plan.
ICMM members commit to 'Target Zero' fatalities; member operations recorded 31 occupational fatalities globally in 2023, down from 33 in 2022.
85% of ICMM members' high-consequence tailings facilities achieved full compliance with the Global Industry Standard on Tailings Management (GISTM).
ICMM is piloting Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) reporting frameworks across 12 member sites to standardize environmental accounting.
ICMM members have committed to double the use of recycled secondary metals in their processing plants by 2035 to reduce resource intensity.
